data.title: Visa, Mastercard merchant fees violate antitrust law, U.K. court rules
data.description:
Visa (NYSE:V) and Mastercard's (NYSE:MA) default multilateral interchange fees came under fire Friday after London's Competition Appeal Tribunal ruled they violate European antitrust law, in a big win for the hundreds of merchants who filed complaints about such "excessive" fees.

The tribunal held the liability phase of the case early last year. A separate decision on whether retailers passed any of the alleged excess charges on to consumers is still to come.

V and MA's multilateral interchange fees are charges that stores have to pay to card-issuing banks every time a customer transacts.

The payment network giants disagreed with the U.K. court's decision and planned to seek permission to appeal, Reuters reported, citing company spokespeople.

The merchants were represented by law firm Scott + Scott in the case.

Meanwhile, in midafternoon trading, shares of MA and V gained 1% and 0.9%, respectively.

data.title: Wall Street is mixed after record highs as Trump ends trade talks with Canada
data.description:
Wall Street’s major averages are mixed on Friday after both the S&P 500 (SP500) and Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) notched fresh all-time trading highs.

The benchmark S&P 500 S&P 500 (SP500) is near flat, the blue-chip the Dow (DJI) has advanced 0.5%, and the tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) is slipped by 0.2%.

U.S. Treasury yields inched up across the curve with the shorter-end U.S. 2 Year Treasury yield (US2Y) moving up 2 basis points to 3.74% while the longer-end U.S. 10 Year Treasury yield (US10Y) has pushed up 4 basis points to 4.28%.

Looking out at how each S&P sector is performing and it can be observed that eight of the 11 segments are in positive territory as Industrials leads the way. At the other end of the spectrum Energy has suffered the most so far. 

On the economic front, May's core PCE Price Index came in at +0.2% M/M versus the +0.1% consensus and +0.1% prior (unrevised) level, and +2.7% Y/Y compared to the +2.6% consensus and +2.6% prior (revised from +2.5%). 

PCE Price Index: +0.1% M/M vs. +0.1% consensus and +0.1% prior (unrevised), and +2.3% Y/Y vs. +2.3% consensus and +2.2% prior (revised from +2.1%). 

Also the U.S. consumer sentiment gauge came in at 60.7 in June's final estimate, compared with the mid-month reading of 60.5 and May's 52.2, according to data released by the University of Michigan on Friday.

Moreover, on the geopolitical trade front, the U.S. and China finalized a trade deal. The White House also noted that it has imminent plans to reach agreements with 10 major trading partners. 

Additionally, U.S. President Donald Trump on Friday said he was ending all trade discussions with Canada, effective immediately, after claiming that the country had imposed a digital services tax on U.S. technology companies.

“Yesterday President Trump confirmed that the US and China have formally signed a new trade agreement, bringing months of negotiations to a close. The deal cements the framework established in recent Geneva meetings, transforming the previous handshake consensus into a binding agreement. While details remain sparse, the announcement removed another layer of uncertainty from the global risk environment. Investors welcomed the confirmation as a positive signal for supply chains and global trade, even if the implementation timeline remains vague,” Trade Nation's David Morrison said.

As for stocks that are on the move, shares of Nike (NKE) have popped up by 15.1% as Wall Street sees early strides in the company’s turnaround — from trimming bloated inventory and tightening supply chain gaps to doubling down on its core growth engines: running and basketball.

data.title: Trump says ending all trade talks with Canada
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump on Friday said he was ending all trade discussions with Canada, effective immediately, after claiming that the country had imposed a digital services tax on U.S. technology companies. 

Wall Street pared some gains after Trump's post on his Truth Social service. Here are some exchange-traded funds that track the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500): (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), and (NYSEARCA:SPXU).

And here are some Canada-linked ETFs:  (NYSEARCA:EWC), (NYSEARCA:FXC), (NYSEARCA:FLCA), and (BATS:BBCA).

data.title: SA Asks: What are the best positioned shipping stocks right now?
data.description:
Which shipping stocks are best positioned to navigate the Middle East crisis?

Seeking Alpha analysts Soroya Investments and Melissa Tucker offer their thoughts. 

Soroya Investments: Threats of closing the Hormuz strait and the jamming of GPS signals disrupt the supply side, causing fewer ships to be available. VLCC [Very Large Crude Carrier] rates have increased dramatically recently. Tankers and gas carriers have already benefited from this imbalance. 

Well-positioned actors include Frontline (NYSE:FRO) and BW LPG (NYSE:BWLP) (OTCPK:BWLLY). The low order book-to-fleet ratio also aids the former. Besides, China relies on Iranian imports for nearly one-eighth of its oil and will lose heavily if the Hormuz strait were to close. They will not allow that to happen. Even though active warfare between Iran and Israel seems to have paused, uncertainty remains.

Melissa Tucker: VLGC [Very Large Gas Carrier] stocks such as Dorian LPG (NYSE:LPG) and BW LPG (NYSE:BWLP) are the best positioned to benefit from the Middle East crisis. VLGC rates were already trending higher before the Iranian crisis, and Iran exports about 7.5% of global volumes through short-haul trade to India and China. 

Recent attacks on refineries will reduce the amount Iran can export, and those volumes could be compensated with higher U.S. exports, increasing ton miles. Moreover, congestion in the Hormuz Strait could increase inefficiencies and rates. There is no need for further escalation for those stocks to shine, as the damage to the refineries is already done and will take several months to repair. Even with the ceasefire, rates haven’t tumbled.

  • Top Oil and Gas Storage and Transportation Stocks
  • Top Marine Transportation Stocks

data.title: Campbell's Q3 beat overshadowed by cautious forecast and GIS fallout
data.description:
Weighed down by General Mills’ (GIS) gloomy outlook and the company’s own conservative profit outlook for FY25, shares of The Campbell’s Company (NASDAQ:CPB) set a new low on Friday after a three-day losing streak.

Campbell’s (NASDAQ:CPB) FQ3 results  exceeded expectations, with a 4% improvement in sales and 2 cent erosion in profit. But the company’s profit margin was squeezed by 110 basis points, and FY25 adjusted earnings are now expected to be at the low end of its guidance range, excluding any negative impact from tariffs. Campbell’s (NASDAQ:CPB) targets a $0.03 to $0.05 tariff headwind to earnings, which was not factored into its EPS target of $2.95 to $3.05 versus $2.98 estimates.

Campbell’s (CPB) shares were pressured, however, by a gloomy assessment of the consumer environment from packaged food peer General Mills (GIS) earlier this week.

The company said it expects net sales to be down 1% to up 1% this year and a 10% to 15% drop in adjusted operating profit and EPS.

But while the negative reaction in General Mills’ (GIS) stock could be attributed more to idiosyncratic factors rather than sector issues in the broader consumer market, shares within the packaged food sector all came under pressure in sympathy, including Campbell’s (CPB).

At $30.81 per share, Campbell’s shares are flashing oversold. Short interest of 6.41% is comparable to other packaged food names, including General Mills (GIS), Kraft Heinz (KHC), and Post (POST).

data.title: MP Materials, rare earth stocks sink as China agrees to lift restrictions
data.description:
Rare earth producers MP Materials (NYSE:MP), USA Rare Earth (NASDAQ:USAR) and Critical Metals (NASDAQ:CRML) -7.3%, -12.1% and -12.3%, respectively, in Friday's trading after China said it would approve export applications for rare earth minerals to the U.S., potentially easing a major irritant in the trade negotiations between the two countries.

Officials from the U.S. and China confirmed the framework of an agreement reached last month after the countries slapped stiff import tariffs on each other.

Shares of rare earth companies had gained in the previous session following a Wall Street Journal report that Chinese authorities were continuing to drag out approval of Western companies' requests for rare earth magnets.

China is responsible for ~70% of the world's rare earth volumes dug up from mines, making them a potent tool in its trade negotiations against the U.S.; China produced 270K metric tons of rare earths in 2024, doubling its output over five years, while the U.S. produced 45K tons, according to data from the U.S. Geological Survey.

The U.S. also has little capacity to refine rare earths, and often need to send them to be refined in China.

data.title: Nvidia acquires Canadian AI startup CentML - report
data.description:
Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) has acquired Toronto-based AI startup CentML, The Logic reported, citing a person familiar with the matter. 

The acquisition was a significant outcome for CentML and its investors, the report added.

Nvidia declined to comment in an email to Seeking Alpha.

CentML CEO Gennady Pekhimenko is now a senior director for AI software at Nvidia, while chief technology officer, or CTO, Sam Wang and COO Akbar Nurlybayev have taken managerial roles at the AI chipmaker, according to their LinkedIn profiles.

Nvidia also brought on at least 15 other engineers and two interns this month who formerly worked at the startup, as per LinkedIn profile data, the report added.

CentML had raised about $30.9M in total in venture capital, according to PitchBook data, the report noted.

The company will cease operations and its AI-optimization services would not be available as of July 17, as per a message Nurlybayev sent customers on June 17 that was posted in the company’s community Slack channel and on a post on X, formerly Twitter.

Not all of CentML’s employees are joining Nvidia. In a LinkedIn post last week, one CentML salesperson noted that his role and others had been eliminated in an “organizational restructuring,” according to the report.

In early June, CentML dissolved its federal corporation and reincorporated under British Columbia law, the report noted.

CentML already had collaborations with Nvidia. The U.S. tech giant participated in CentML's $27M seed round in October 2023. In November 2024, CentML announced it had enrolled in Nvidia’s accelerator program, the report added.

CentML was founded in 2022 and was one of several companies promising to help clients run AI systems more efficiently and cheaply. The company's software slotted in between users’ AI models and the chips powering them, and tapped under-used hardware capacity and employed other methods to make the systems run better. The startup developed its own compiler, which translates code for applications to a machine language which processors can understand, the report noted.

At Nvidia, CentML’s former engineers will be working on software, the report added.

The acquisition comes at a time when Nvidia's stock is on a roll. Year-to-date, the stock has jumped about 17%. The shares have been in the green this week and are expected to end Friday with gains. As of 12:17 p.m. ET, the stock had climbed about 1.71% on Friday.

The AI company, which currently has a market cap of about $3.78T, is in the running to hit $4T market cap this year and could reach the $5T milestone in the next 18 months, as the AI revolution looks to be accelerating, according to Wedbush.

data.title: UBS sees fresh sizzle in McDonald's after pullback
data.description:
After an 11% retracement since mid-May, McDonald’s (NYSE:MCD) shares are attractively priced as trends improve and as new products, value initiatives and marketing plans deliver same store sales growth, says UBS analyst Dennis Geiger in a note to clients.

McDonald’s (NYSE:MCD) shares have been on the defensive since May 20 as investors bailed out of the stock amid concerns about consumer spending habits, backlash on price hikes, and the proliferation of weight-loss medication that has undermined fast food chains and snacking categories.

But while Geiger recognizes quick service sales trends remain pressured, he anticipates share gains as U.S. and international trends improve, particularly if the risk-on trade cools.

“McDonald’s appears well positioned to deliver solid [same store sales] in H2 2025 driven by new products, value initiatives, and marketing plans that should resonate, even as lower and middle income spending pressures could linger,” Geiger says.

The company has also enacted key initiatives that will support share gains and stronger same store sales in the second half of this year, including menu innovations, value meal deals, marketing promotions, expanded summer hours, and, the introduction of CosMc’s beverages into McDonald’s locations.

UBS is bullish on McDonald’s with a Buy rating and $350 price target that assumes a 22% upside to Thursday’s closing price. Geiger’s Buy rating is consistent with the average rating among Wall Street analysts, while Seeking Alpha analysts and Quant rating have maintained a Hold rating on McDonald’s (NYSE:MCD) since at least October 2024.

data.title: Simon Property acquires interest in retail and parking at Brickell City Centre
data.description:
Retail REIT Simon Property Group (NYSE:SPG) announced on Friday the acquisition of Swire Properties' interest in the retail and parking at Brickell City Centre.

Brickell City Centre is a mixed-use property that spans ~5M sq. ft.

Prior to the acquisition, Simon owned a 25%, non-managing interest in the retail at Brickell City Centre, and, following this transaction, will wholly own and manage the asset.

data.title: WHO report says COVID-19 likely originated in transfer from wild animals to humans
data.description:
A new report from a World Health Organization panel has determined that COVID-19 originated in China likely through "zoonotic spillover," when a virus or bacteria is transferred from a wild animal host to humans.

That animal host, the Scientific Advisory Group for the Origins of Novel Pathogens suggested, were bats.

However, WHO Director-General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus noted that other hypotheses are still on the table, including one that the virus escaped in a lab leak. That is a reference to the theory that the COVID pandemic started with a leak of the virus from the Wuhan Institute of Virology.

"Without information to fully assess the nature of the work on coronaviruses in Wuhan laboratories, nor information about the conditions under which this work was done, it is not possible for SAGO to assess whether the first human infection(s) may have resulted due to a research related event or breach in laboratory biosafety," the report says.

The international public health association also took aim at the Chinese government for not being forthcoming with information it had asked for.

"WHO requested that China share hundreds of genetic sequences from individuals with COVID-19 early in the pandemic, more detailed information about the animals sold at markets in Wuhan, and information on work done and biosafety conditions at laboratories in Wuhan," a news release states. "To date, China has not shared this information either with SAGO or WHO."

The report noted that the panel "is not currently able to conclude exactly when, where and how SARS-CoV-2 first entered the human population."
